title: Porting Libraries to Crystal with AI
summary: The article discusses how AI tools enable developers to port open-source libraries to the Crystal programming language, which has a smaller ecosystem than alternatives. It outlines the practical process of selecting reference implementations, deciding between porting and binding approaches, and respecting licensing requirements—making it viable to expand Crystal's library ecosystem through AI-assisted porting.
content:
This post was originally written in Japanese and translated into English by the author using ChatGPT. The original post is available here.
AI coding tools have become much better, and I now write code by hand far less often than before. I use GitHub Copilot in VSCode through the OSS benefit program, and recently I have also been trying Claude Code and Codex.
Compared with other programming languages, Crystal does not have a very large library ecosystem.
However, many open source libraries have licenses that allow porting, and AI can provide a lot of help with porting work. Because of this, when a library is missing, it is becoming realistic to consider porting as an option.
In this article, I want to put into words and record what I actually do when porting libraries.
The first step is to choose the library to use as a reference.
In Crystal, compared with C or Go, a higher level of abstraction is often expected. On the other hand, Crystal cannot use metaprogramming as freely as Ruby or Python.
In that sense, Crystal is a language somewhere in the middle, and it is not always possible to choose a single reference library. Sometimes it is necessary to look at multiple libraries and think about what would be best. In my case, I often look at active Rust and Go projects, while using Ruby and Python APIs as references. When the reference is written in Rust, binding to it may sometimes be better than porting it.
That kind of separation is the first step. If I decide that porting is the right approach, I move on. There may also be cases where porting and bindings are mixed.
The most important point is the license. I check whether the original library is under a license that allows porting, such as MIT, BSD, Apache-2.0, or another license whose conditions I can comply with. I try to make the new library inherit the license terms of the original.
I also clearly state where the original project came from. In my case, I add the reference repository as a whole using git submodu

zh.title: 使用AI将库移植到Crystal
zh.summary: 本文讨论了AI工具如何使开发人员能够将开源库移植到生态系统较小的Crystal编程语言。它概述了选择参考实现、在移植和绑定方法之间做出决定以及尊重许可要求的实际过程，使通过AI辅助的移植来扩展Crystal库生态系统成为可能。

title: The Ultimate Guide to Fixing AppImages on Ubuntu 24.04 LTS
summary: This guide explains why AppImages fail on Ubuntu 24.04 LTS due to FUSE version incompatibility, AppArmor security restrictions, and missing execution permissions, then provides step-by-step solutions. The fix is essential for Ubuntu users whose preferred applications became unusable after system upgrades.
content:
If you have recently upgraded to Ubuntu 24.04 (Noble Numbat) or 22.04 LTS, you might have noticed a frustrating trend: your favorite AppImages—like Oodles TimeTracker, Discord, or BalenaEtcher—simply refuse to open. You double-click the file, the loading icon spins for a second, and then... nothing.
This isn't a bug in the applications themselves, but rather a result of Ubuntu’s evolving security architecture and library transitions. In this post, we’ll break down exactly why this happens and provide a permanent, secure fix.
Why AppImages "Break" on Modern Ubuntu


There are three primary culprits behind the "silent failure" of AppImages on newer versions of Ubuntu:
 The FUSE 2 vs. FUSE 3 Gap: Ubuntu now ships with FUSE 3. Most older AppImages were built using FUSE 2 (libfuse2). Since these versions are not cross-compatible, the AppImage cannot "mount" itself to run.
 AppArmor Restrictions (The 24.04 Factor): Ubuntu 24.04 introduced a security hardening measure that restricts "unprivileged user namespaces." Many apps (especially those built on Electron) require these namespaces for sandboxing. If AppArmor doesn't recognize the app, it kills the process immediately.
 Missing Execution Permissions: By default, Linux treats downloaded files as non-executable for security.
Phase 1: The Essential Foundation


Before diving into advanced security profiles, let’s handle the basics.
1. Install the Compatibility Library


You need the library that allows FUSE 2 apps to talk to your modern system.
For Ubuntu 24.04 / 24.10:
sudo apt update && sudo apt install libfuse2t64

For Ubuntu 22.04:
sudo apt update && sudo apt install libfuse2

2. Grant Execution Permissions


The system must be told that this file is allowed to run as a program.
Terminal: chmod +x /path/to/your-app.AppImage


GUI: Right-click the file > Properties > Permissions > Check "Allow executing file as program."







  
  
  Phase 2: The Pro Fix (AppArmor Profiles)


While you could disable Ubuntu’s security

zh.title: Ubuntu 24.04 LTS上修复AppImages的终极指南
zh.summary: 本指南解释了为什么AppImages在Ubuntu 24.04 LTS上因FUSE版本不兼容、AppArmor安全限制和缺少执行权限而失败，然后提供分步解决方案。这个修复对于在系统升级后其首选应用程序变得无法使用的Ubuntu用户至关重要。

title: What Is Apache Polaris? Why Open Data Catalogs Matter and How to Use Them with AWS
summary: Apache Polaris is an open-source data catalog compliant with Apache Iceberg REST specifications that enables cross-platform management of data tables without vendor lock-in. It operates at a different architectural layer than AWS Glue or Snowflake catalogs, making it relevant for organizations building lakehouse architectures around Iceberg.
content:
Original Japanese article: Apache Polarisとは何か？オープンなデータカタログが求められる理由とAWSとの組み合わせ方を整理する
I'm Aki, an AWS Community Builder (@jitepengin).
In recent years, lakehouse architectures centered around Apache Iceberg have been rapidly expanding.
By placing Iceberg tables on object storage such as S3, it has become possible to query the same data from multiple engines such as Athena, Snowflake, Spark, Trino, and Dremio.
“Where should data be placed, and which engine should be used for analysis?” to “Where should data ownership reside, and which catalog should be used to unify governance?”
Amid this trend, Apache Polaris has been attracting attention in recent years.
Multiple vendors—including Dremio, AWS, Google, Microsoft, and Confluent—are contributing to it, and it is positioned as an “open catalog” that enables cross-platform management of Iceberg tables while avoiding vendor lock-in.
In this article, I would like to think through the following:
What Apache Polaris is
Why open data catalogs are required
Differences from AWS Glue Data Catalog
Differences from Snowflake Horizon Catalog
How responsibilities should be divided when combining with AWS
In conclusion, Apache Polaris is not something that competes with AWS Glue Catalog or Snowflake Horizon Catalog; rather, they are catalogs that operate at different layers.
It may be easier to understand Apache Polaris as a component that enables an architecture such as:
“The data itself resides in AWS, the catalog is open, and analysis engines are selected based on use cases.”
Apache Polaris is an open-source catalog implementation compliant with the Apache Iceberg REST Catalog specification.
Official site:
https://polaris.apache.org/
What Polaris aims to achieve is a common metadata and governance foundation in a lakehouse centered around Iceberg tables.
A major characteristic is that it is not tied to any specific query engine or cloud vendor, and anyone can access it using the same specification via REST APIs.



Feature
Descript

zh.title: 什么是Apache Polaris？为什么开放数据目录很重要以及如何在AWS中使用它们
zh.summary: Apache Polaris是一个符合Apache Iceberg REST规范的开源数据目录，可以跨平台管理数据表而无需供应商锁定。它在与AWS Glue或Snowflake目录不同的架构层运行，使其与为Iceberg构建数据湖屋架构的组织相关。

title: Why AI Makes Software Fundamentals More Expensive Than Ever
summary: Contrary to claims that AI makes engineering skills obsolete, the author argues that strong fundamentals are now more critical because AI amplifies both good and bad practices—clean architecture makes AI highly productive, while messy code becomes prohibitively expensive to maintain. AI acts as a multiplier, not a replacement, requiring solid engineering foundations.
content:
Introduction


There is a message circulating in the dev world that's intended to be comforting, but it's actually a bit of a trap. It’s the idea that in the age of LLMs, your hard-earned engineering skills are becoming obsolete. That we are moving toward a "Specs-to-Code" world where you just write a prompt, the AI spits out the app, and you never have to look at the "cheap" code underneath.
I’m here to tell you the opposite: Software fundamentals matter now more than they actually ever have.
In fact, after teaching "AI for Real Engineers", I’ve realized that if you treat code as cheap, you'll quickly find yourself drowning in "software entropy."
We’ve all tried it. You give the AI a spec, it generates code. You find a bug; you update the spec and run the "compiler" again.
What happens next?
The first version is okay.
The second version is slightly worse.
By the fifth iteration, you have absolute garbage.
This isn't coding; it's "Voodoo Coding". The idea that we can ignore the code and let it manage itself is a recipe for disaster. As John Ousterhout defines it in "A Philosophy of Software Design" bad code is Complex Code anything that makes the system hard to understand and modify.
If you can't change a codebase without causing bugs, it’s a bad codebase. And guess what? AI struggles most in bad codebases.
The AI doesn't replace engineers; it multiplies them. But a multiplier only works if the base number isn't zero.
If you have a codebase with:
Good Architecture: Designed for change.
Clear Abstractions: Hiding complexity.
Tests & Feedback: Fast loops for the AI to learn from.
Then the AI does really, really well. If your code is a mess, the AI will just help you make a bigger mess, faster. Bad code is the most expensive it's ever been because it prevents you from taking the bounty that AI offers.
How do we avoid the "garbage loop"? We go back to the old books and apply them to the new paradigm.
The AI didn't do what I wanted


In the "Pragmatic Programmer", they s

zh.title: 为什么AI让软件基础知识变得比以往任何时候都更加昂贵
zh.summary: 与声称AI使工程技能过时的说法相反，作者辩称强大的基础知识现在更加关键，因为AI会放大良好和不良的实践——干净的架构使AI高度高效，而凌乱的代码维护成本变得过高。AI是一个乘数而非替代品，需要扎实的工程基础。

title: The 6 MCP tools in Hashlock Markets — what each one does and when an agent calls it
summary: Hashlock Markets provides six MCP tools designed for autonomous agent calls: two tools for RFQ-based price discovery and four for HTLC settlement without escrow or bridges. Understanding these tools is essential for teams integrating AI agents with Hashlock's decentralized trading platform.
content:
When you wire an AI agent into Hashlock Markets, it gets exactly six MCP tools. That is the whole surface. There is no SDK to import, no fifty-method client to pin to a major version, no separate auth library. Six tools, one protocol, and a trade either settles atomically or it doesn't.
This post is the FAQ that I get most often from teams evaluating the surface for the first time: what are the six tools, what does each one do, and when does the agent actually call it?
The six tools split cleanly into two halves:
Two RFQ-side tools — these are about price discovery without information leakage.
Four HTLC-side tools — these are about settlement without escrow or bridges.
Both halves are designed to be called directly by an autonomous agent. Every tool takes typed inputs, returns typed outputs, and corresponds to a single onchain state transition (or, in the RFQ case, a single signed off-chain action). Nothing in the surface assumes a human in the loop.
create_rfq — post a sealed-bid intent


A taker calls this when it knows what it wants but doesn't know the price yet. The arguments describe the trade: which asset out, which asset in, the maximum slippage you're willing to tolerate, the deadline, and the chains involved. The output is an RFQ identifier that makers can quote against.
The "sealed-bid" part is the structural property: the bid is not broadcast to a public book the moment it's created. It's posted into a Request-for-Quote venue where makers see only the parameters they need to price the trade. Public order books can't do this — by the time a price is on a public book, the leakage has already happened. For agent flows where the same AI decides what to trade and how to trade it, the leakage difference compounds quickly.
respond_rfq — quote against an open RFQ


A maker (an agent or a desk running its own MCP-connected service) calls this with a quote against an open RFQ: the price, the size, and a signature committing to the maker's side of the trade. The ta

zh.title: Hashlock Markets中的6个MCP工具——每个工具的作用和何时被代理调用
zh.summary: Hashlock Markets提供了六个为自主代理调用而设计的MCP工具：两个用于基于RFQ的价格发现，四个用于无需托管或跨链桥接的HTLC结算。理解这些工具对于将AI代理与Hashlock去中心化交易平台集成的团队至关重要。

title: Architecting for Modern Finance: Deconstructing the Financial Services Ecosystem - Part 2
summary: Part 2 covers essential DevOps practices for modern financial services: developer experience optimization, shift-left testing, cloud-based development environments, CI/CD automation, and AI-driven operations. The article guides teams building financial infrastructure that needs to balance deployment velocity with reliability and security.
content:
Developer Experience (DX): The New Competitive Advantage


Developer Experience (DX) is the overall experience developers have while building, testing, and deploying software.
High-performing companies like Netflix and Amazon invest heavily in DX because:
Faster developers = faster business delivery
Better tools = fewer production issues
Good DX includes:
Fast local setup
Integrated tooling
Clear feedback loops
Shift Left means moving testing, security, and quality checks earlier in the development lifecycle.
Instead of fixing issues in production:
Detect bugs during coding
Fix vulnerabilities before commit
Benefits:
Reduced cost
Faster releases
Better quality
Cloud-based development environments allow developers to code without local setup.
Instead of traditional IDEs, teams now use:
GitHub Codespaces
Gitpod
AWS Cloud9
JetBrains Space
These provide:
Pre-configured environments
Faster onboarding
Consistent setups
CI/CD pipeline is an automated workflow that builds, tests, and deploys applications.
Popular tools:
Jenkins
GitHub Actions
GitLab CI/CD
AWS CodeBuild
Azure DevOps Pipelines
Pipelines integrate:
Build tools
Test frameworks
Security scans
Deployment automation
Performance testing
Code quality tools analyze code for maintainability, bugs, and standards.
Tools include:
SonarQube
Codacy
CodeClimate
DeepSource
PMD
Application security tools identify vulnerabilities in code before deployment.
Snyk
Checkmarx
Fortify
WhiteSource
Contrast Security
Deployment model defines where and how applications run.
1. On-Premise
2. Cloud VM (EC2)
3. Containers (ECS, Kubernetes)
4. Serverless (Lambda)
Additional Options
There is no single best solution—only context-driven decisions.
Top companies like Google and Microsoft:
Use microservices + containers
Invest in platform engineering
Standardize pipelines
AI in DevOps uses machine learning to improve development, testing, and operations
Top 5 use cases:
Intelligent code suggestions
Automated test generation
Vulnerability detecti

zh.title: 现代金融架构：分解金融服务生态系统 - 第2部分
zh.summary: 第2部分涵盖现代金融服务的关键DevOps实践：开发者体验优化、左移测试、基于云的开发环境、CI/CD自动化和AI驱动运营。该文章为构建需要平衡部署速度、可靠性和安全性的金融基础设施的团队提供指导。

title: "AI-Accelerated Development" is Just a Cute Name for Not Knowing What You're Doing
summary: The author satirizes the tech industry's embrace of vague AI-related titles like "AIOps Engineer" without substance, contrasting superficial API-wrapping with actual AIOps—a rigorous mathematical discipline. The piece advocates for returning to fundamental system knowledge rather than adopting buzzwords.
content:
Calling Yourself an "AIOps Engineer" Because You Wrapped an API is Like Claiming Michelin Status for Microwaving a Hot Pocket.
Recently, I decided to dedicate some serious time to learning the current state of AI. As a DevOps engineer, my daily life is governed by strict logic, deep system automation, and knowing exactly how the plumbing works. But diving into the modern AI hype cycle? It feels less like acquiring a new technical skill and more like staring into a bottomless pit of buzzwords.
If you spend five minutes on tech social media right now, you will inevitably encounter an army of people proudly wearing titles like "AI Automation Engineer" or "AIOps Specialist."
Here is the punchline: actual AIOps—a term coined by Gartner back in 2016—is a rigorous, deeply mathematical discipline. It relies on big data and machine learning to automate complex IT processes like predictive scaling, anomaly detection, and root cause analysis to keep enterprise servers from catching fire.
It is not writing a five-line Python script that pings ChatGPT.
Instead, the title has been hijacked by a new breed of developers I like to call "Wrapper Engineers." They don't care how the sausage is made; they just want to print the receipt.
This reality hit me like a brick during my recent laps around the vendor floors at KubeCon and AWS re:Invent. If you’ve been, you know exactly what I’m talking about.
The panel ends, the Q&A session begins, and someone confidently strides up to the microphone. They then proceed to ask a question so logically self-evident that it borders on philosophical performance art. The answer is obvious. The documentation is pristine. Yet, they ask it anyway, and suddenly half the room is nodding along, stroking their chins as if this person just unlocked the secrets of the cosmos.
It made me realize something painful about our current ecosystem: a massive chunk of the tech industry has completely stopped caring about what happens under the hood.
We are simply hacki

zh.title: "AI加速开发"不过是掩盖无知的漂亮名称
zh.summary: 作者讽刺科技行业对模糊AI相关职位（如"AIOps工程师"）的盲目拥抱，缺乏实质内容，对比肤浅的API包装与严谨的AIOps数学学科。该文章倡导回归基础系统知识，而不是追随时髦术语。

title: Why Standard Indexes Fail: The Architecture of the Covering Index
summary: Standard database indexes create a hidden bottleneck by requiring lookups across both index and main table, while covering indexes solve this by storing all needed columns in the index itself. This optimization is critical at scale where query latency directly affects application stability.
content:
In my last article, I broke down how and why to use indexes wisely for efficient lookups and data retrieval by identifying fields to index and which not to index. Can read that here.
But adding a standard index in some cases is half the battle.
Adding an index to a column creates a separate, organized B-Tree for that column. And at the bottom of the tree (leaf node) is the pointer. That pointer tells the database engine where the full row lives on the heap. The heap is the main table where the full rows live. It is unsorted, massive, and slow to search as it forces a full-table scan. Indexes help to make data retrieval faster.
Standard indexes make searching fast, but they introduce a hidden bottleneck. Let’s look at a real-world example to see how and when to use a Covering Index to fix it.

Spotify “Recently Played” Screen
Think of any music streaming platform like Spotify or Apple Music. Every time you open the app, it instantly fetches your most recently played tracks, around 10 tracks initially.
The query looks like this:
SELECT track_id, played_at 
FROM listen_history 
WHERE user_id = '018dc336-1234...' 
ORDER BY played_at DESC 
LIMIT 10;

Without an index, the query is forced to do a full-table scan. Let’s say Spotify has 100 million records on its listen_history table, which means for each query.
100 million rows will be scanned just to isolate this single user’s history
Then it is sorted in memory.
And then the top 10 i
s returned.
At scale, this doesn’t just increase response times. This manual RAM sort will choke your disk I/O, exhaust your connection pool, and eventually bring down your application with 500 server errors.

Execution Time roughly 217ms with no index as a result of the Parallel Sequential Scan with 1,000,000 rows scanned. (Nano Banana for sharpening text in the terminal)
CREATE INDEX idx_user_history ON listen_history (user_id);

SELECT track_id, played_at 
FROM listen_history 
WHERE user_id = '018dc336-1234...' 
ORDER BY played_at DESC 
L

zh.title: 标准索引为何失效：覆盖索引的架构
zh.summary: 标准数据库索引通过需要在索引和主表之间进行查找来创建隐藏瓶颈，而覆盖索引通过在索引中存储所有需要的列来解决这个问题。这种优化在大规模系统中至关重要，因为查询延迟直接影响应用稳定性。

title: TestSprite Review: AI Testing Agent untuk Developer Indonesia — Apakah Worth It?
summary: TestSprite is an AI testing agent that automatically generates, runs, and maintains test cases integrated with AI coding tools like Cursor, significantly accelerating test coverage for both frontend and backend. For SaaS developers, it addresses testing bottlenecks by reducing manual test authoring and maintenance overhead.
content:
Sebagai developer yang aktif mengerjakan proyek SaaS di Indonesia, masalah testing selalu jadi bottleneck terbesar dalam workflow saya. Manual testing memakan waktu, automation framework butuh maintenance intensif setiap kali UI berubah, dan sprint jadi lambat.
Ketika saya mendengar tentang TestSprite — sebuah AI testing agent yang diklaim bisa otomatis generate, run, dan maintain test cases — saya langsung penasaran untuk mencobanya secara langsung.

TestSprite terintegrasi langsung dengan Cursor — terlihat test berjalan otomatis via MCP
Artikel ini adalah review jujur dari pengalaman saya menggunakan TestSprite di project nyata.
TestSprite adalah autonomous AI testing agent yang dirancang sebagai "verification layer" dalam agentic development workflow. Dia bisa:
Parse requirements dari PRD atau langsung dari codebase via MCP server
Generate test cases otomatis untuk UI flows, API logic, dan edge cases
Run tests di ephemeral cloud sandbox
Auto-patch feedback langsung ke coding agent (Cursor, Claude Code, dll.)
Yang paling menarik adalah integrasinya dengan AI coding tools populer — TestSprite hadir sebagai lapisan verifikasi yang menutup gap antara "AI menulis kode" dan "kode siap production."
Proses signup cukup mudah — tersedia free tier tanpa perlu kartu kredit. Dashboard-nya clean dan intuitif. Saya connect ke project Node.js + React yang sedang saya kerjakan.
Yang pertama saya coba adalah fitur Unified Batch Generation — satu klik untuk generate test coverage sekaligus untuk frontend dan backend. Hasilnya cukup mengejutkan: dalam beberapa menit, TestSprite sudah generate belasan test case yang mencakup berbagai user flow utama tanpa saya perlu tulis satu baris test code pun.
Test generation-nya cepat. TestSprite crawl aplikasi dan langsung memahami flow utama. Untuk project dengan codebase yang cukup besar, ini significant time saver.
Akurasi test case-nya bagus untuk happy path scenarios. Untuk edge cases yang lebih kompleks, masih perlu sedikit fine-tuning m

zh.title: TestSprite评测：AI测试代理对开发者是否值得投入？
zh.summary: TestSprite是一个AI测试代理，可自动生成、运行和维护测试用例，并与Cursor等AI编程工具集成，显著加快前端和后端的测试覆盖率。对于SaaS开发者，它通过减少手动测试编写和维护开销来解决测试瓶颈。

title: How I Built a Production-Ready Voucher Management System with Laravel 12 and Vue 3
summary: A developer describes building VMMS, a production-ready voucher management system replacing manual processing with automated tracking and role-based dashboards using Laravel 12 and Vue 3. The system includes features like holiday-aware deadlines, audit trails, and automated notifications, demonstrating full-stack development best practices.
content:
The Problem


I'm a CS student in the Philippines. During my time observing how 
Voucher processing is still done manually almost everywhere. 
No tracking. No visibility. The client has no idea where their 
I kept thinking — this is exactly the kind of problem software 
VMMS stands for Voucher Management & Monitoring System.
It handles the entire voucher request process — from the moment 
No paper. No manual routing. No "what's the status of my request?"
I went with what I know best:
Laravel 12 for the backend — routing, queues, and email jobs
Vue 3 (Composition API) for the frontend — reactive and clean
Inertia.js v2 — so I don't have to build a separate API
Tailwind CSS + Vuetify 3 — for UI components and styling
MySQL 8.0 — for the relational data and complex queries
Resend — for transactional emails
There are three separate panels depending on your role:
Admin
Staff
Client
Some other things worth mentioning:
Deadlines are holiday-aware — staff don't get penalized for 
non-working days
Every action is logged in an audit trail
PDF and Excel exports on all reports
Automated email notifications at every stage
Date queries across MySQL and MariaDB
The pipeline tracker
Holiday-aware deadlines
🔴 Live demo: https://vmms-app-production.up.railway.app/login
Credentials if you want to explore:
Admin: admin@vmms.demo | Password: Admin@Demo123!
staff.infra@vmms.demo | staff.budget@vmms.demo | staff.procurement@vmms.demo | staff.supply@vmms.demo | staff.executive@vmms.demo | Password: Staff@Demo123!
client@vmms.demo | Password: Client@Demo123!
I'm selling VMMS in two versions:
🔒 Obfuscated / Protected — $99
👨‍💻 Full Source Code — $300
Both versions come with documentation, a quick start guide, 
👉 https://getvmms.gumroad.com/l/zeroqz
Happy to answer questions in the comments — whether about 
the build, the tech stack, or anything else. 🚀

zh.title: 使用 Laravel 12 和 Vue 3 构建生产就绪的代金券管理系统
zh.summary: 一名开发者描述了构建 VMMS（生产就绪的代金券管理系统）的过程，该系统使用 Laravel 12 和 Vue 3 替代手动处理流程，实现自动化跟踪和基于角色的仪表板。该系统包括假日感知截止日期、审计日志和自动通知等功能，展示了全栈开发的最佳实践。

title: I built a Chrome extension that downloads every image on a page — here's what I learned about Shadow DOM, CSS backgrounds, and Manifest V3
summary: The developer shares technical and product lessons from building a Chrome image-batch-download extension, covering challenges with Shadow DOM and CSS backgrounds under Manifest V3, plus decisions around telemetry and pricing. The extension demonstrates practical browser extension development with a freemium model.
content:
nobody asked about.
Add telemetry from day one — opt-in, anonymous, but I have no idea which features users actually use. Adding it now feels invasive.
Write the test suite earlier. I have ~30% coverage. It's not a disaster, but every refactor is slower than it needs to be.
If you want to see all this in action:
Install: https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/iecgnjidmogebokcfnejncgnelcepffo


Site: https://image-harvest.kyriewen.cn (full feature comparison + GIFs)
Reviews (if it helps you): https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/iecgnjidmogebokcfnejncgnelcepffo/reviews

github：https://github.com/zbw-zbw/image-harvest



Free tier handles most use. Pro adds multi-tab extract, similar-image detection, 4-engine reverse search, color extraction, and tagged collections. One-time payment option because I personally hate subscriptions for utilities.
If you've shipped a Chrome extension and have war stories about MV3, Shadow DOM, or the Side Panel API — drop them in the comments. Solo devs unite. 🙌

zh.title: 我构建了一个可下载页面所有图片的 Chrome 扩展程序——关于 Shadow DOM、CSS 背景和 Manifest V3 的经验
zh.summary: 开发者分享了构建 Chrome 图像批量下载扩展程序的技术和产品经验，涵盖 Manifest V3 下 Shadow DOM 和 CSS 背景的挑战，以及围绕遥测和定价的决策。该扩展程序展示了采用免费增值模式的实用浏览器扩展开发。

title: AAEF v0.6.0: Practical Adoption Readiness Planning Release
summary: AAEF v0.6.0 is a control framework for ensuring agentic AI systems maintain properly authorized, bounded, and evidenced actions rather than relying solely on model output. The release includes adoption planning materials for implementers, operators, compliance teams, and executives, emphasizing accountability when AI agents perform real-world actions.
content:
I’ve published AAEF v0.6.0.
AAEF — Agentic Authority & Evidence Framework — is an action assurance control profile for agentic AI systems.
The central idea is:
Model output is not authority.
When AI systems only generate text, many safety discussions focus on model behavior: accuracy, alignment, explainability, or refusal behavior.
But when AI agents can call tools, access data, delegate work, or perform actions in production systems, another question becomes critical:
Was this action authorized, bounded, attributable, and evidenced?
AAEF focuses on that action layer.
v0.6.0 is a planning and adoption-readiness release. It does not change the current active control and assessment baseline.
This release organizes planning artifacts for:
implementers
operators
legal and compliance teams
security architects
risk owners and executives
It also adds planning material for authorization decision artifacts, implementer quick start guidance, operational responsibility, high-impact production architecture, legal/compliance applicability, and risk owner decision support.
AAEF is not a certification scheme, legal compliance claim, audit opinion, conformity assessment, or equivalence claim with external frameworks.
It is intended as a public-reviewable control profile for delegated authority, policy-enforced action boundaries, and verifiable evidence in agentic AI systems.
Release:
https://github.com/mkz0010/agentic-authority-evidence-framework/releases/tag/v0.6.0
Repository:
https://github.com/mkz0010/agentic-authority-evidence-framework
Feedback and critical review are welcome.

zh.title: AAEF v0.6.0：实用采用就绪规划发布版本
zh.summary: AAEF v0.6.0 是一个控制框架，用于确保代理 AI 系统维护正确授权、有边界和有依据的行为，而不仅仅依赖模型输出。该发布版本包括为实施者、运营者、合规团队和高管提供的采用规划材料，强调当 AI 代理执行现实世界的行为时的问责制。

title: A Beginner's Guide to Apache Airflow 3
summary: Apache Airflow provides a programmatic approach to orchestrating data workflows through Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) and Tasks, enabling data pipelines to manage task dependencies and execution timing. Unlike cron jobs which blindly execute at scheduled times regardless of task completion, Airflow ensures tasks run only after their dependencies complete, preventing data corruption in ETL processes.
content:
If the terms orchestration or Apache Airflow sound like intimidating industry jargon, this article will help you cut through the noise and understand the basics.
data orchestration? In DataOps (Data Operations), it is the underlying system that manages data workflows (such as ETL pipelines) to ensure tasks run at the right time and in the correct sequence.
What is a DAG? A DAG is a model that contains all the tasks to be run. DAG stands for:
Directed meaning tasks have a specific direction.
Acyclic meaning it has no circular dependencies — extraction cannot depend on transformation if transformation depends on extraction.
Graph meaning a collection of tasks (nodes) connected by dependencies (edges).
What is a Task? This is a step in a DAG that describes a single unit of work.

Think of the DAG as an orchestra conductor and the tasks as the instruments. 
The Scheduler submits tasks to the executor and triggers scheduled workflows.
A DAG processor reads DAG files and organizes them in the metadata database.
The Webserver is the Airflow User Interface for inspecting, triggering and debugging the behaviour of DAGs and tasks.
A dedicated folder of DAG files which contains the DAG, is read by the scheduler to figure out which tasks to run and when to run them.
The Metadata Database stores the state of tasks, DAGs and variables.
At this point you might be asking yourself, Why not just use cron jobs? Well, think of cron jobs as an alarm clock and Airflow as a project manager. Cron just runs your script at a certain time with no regard for the task's dependencies. 
extract.py for 12:00 AM and transform.py for 1:30 AM. If extraction takes 40 minutes, Cron will blindly trigger the transformation at 1:30 AM, leading to corrupted data or a crash. 
from airflow.sdk import DAG 
from airflow.providers.standard.operators.python import PythonOperator
from airflow.providers.standard.operators.bash import BashOperator
from datetime import datetime, timedelta 

# Step 1: Define your Pyt

zh.title: Apache Airflow 3 初学者指南
zh.summary: Apache Airflow 通过有向无环图（DAG）和任务提供了一种编程方式来协调数据工作流，使数据管道能够管理任务依赖关系和执行时间。与无论任务是否完成都盲目按计划时间执行的 cron 作业不同，Airflow 确保任务只有在其依赖项完成后才能运行，防止 ETL 流程中的数据损坏。

title: 12 Phrases Your ATS Is Quietly Penalizing — Found by Running 200 Resumes Through 5 Systems
summary: Analysis of 200 resumes processed through five ATS systems identified 12 phrases that consistently reduce screening scores by at least 4 percentile points, including 'responsible for,' 'helped with,' and vague descriptors like 'various.' These problematic phrases fall into three categories: filler verbs that obscure accomplishments, vague nouns that produce no keyword overlap with job descriptions, and unsubstantiated soft skill claims.
content:
The follow-up I owed myself. Last week I reverse-engineered 5 ATS systems and that post hit harder than I expected, partly because it gave readers a mental model but mostly because it made them ask the obvious follow-up question:
Okay, but which words specifically should I delete from my resume?
So I ran another batch — 200 anonymized resumes through 5 different ATS-style scoring pipelines (3 commercial, 2 open-source replicas tuned to match the commercial outputs) and looked for phrases that consistently dropped the score relative to a baseline. Here are the 12 worst offenders.
For each resume I ran two passes: original, and a "phrase-scrubbed" copy where I removed only the candidate phrase and replaced it with a neutral synonym from a controlled list. I logged the score delta. The phrases below produced an average score drop of at least 4 percentile points across all 5 systems, and at least one system flagged the resume into a lower screening tier.
1. "Responsible for"
2. "Helped with" / "Assisted with"
3. "Various" (as in "various technologies", "various clients")
4. "Synergize" / "Synergies"
5. "Innovative solutions"
6. "Team player"
7. "Strong communication skills"
8. "Familiar with [tool]"
9. "Hard worker" / "Detail-oriented"
10. "Multitask" / "Multitasking"
11. "References available upon request"
12. "Proven track record"
If you read the list back to back, the same three failure modes show up:
Filler verbs that hide the real verb. Responsible for, helped with, assisted with. Get rid of the helper verb and lead with the action.
Vague nouns that match nothing in the job description. Various, multiple, several. The ATS scores keyword overlap; vague nouns produce no overlap.
Self-claimed soft skills with no evidence. Team player, hard worker, strong communicator, detail-oriented. None of them match anything the JD asked for.
Pull up your resume and search for those 12 phrases. Removing them takes maybe 20 minutes. The systems I tested moved an average resume up a

zh.title: 12 个短语正被你的 ATS 系统偷偷降分 — 通过 5 个系统处理 200 份简历发现
zh.summary: 对通过五个 ATS 系统处理的 200 份简历的分析发现了 12 个短语，这些短语将筛选得分降低至少 4 个百分点，包括"负责"、"协助"和模糊描述符如"各种"。这些有问题的短语分为三类：隐藏成就的填充动词、与职位描述没有关键词重叠的模糊名词，以及未经证实的软技能声明。

The concept of an 'AI harness'—proper repository setup with documentation, clear architecture, and consistent patterns—can transform an agent's effectiveness from producing fabricated code to shipping production-ready features.

title: Do Workshop Papers at NeurIPS/ICML Actually Help Your PhD Application? Here's What Admissions Committees Actually See
summary: Workshop papers from conferences like NeurIPS and ICML can significantly influence PhD admissions outcomes, but their value depends on positioning and broader application context. A workshop paper serves as a signal amplifier rather than a standalone credential, strengthening applications that already demonstrate research capability while providing limited benefit to weaker candidates. Applicants should understand the informal prestige hierarchy of ML venues and strategically position workshop papers.
content:
TL;DR: You got accepted to the NeurIPS 2025 Workshop on Robustness in Foundation Models.  Congrats — that's genuinely hard.
📖 Reading time: ~28 min
The Problem: You Have a Workshop Paper and You Don't Know What It's Worth
First, Let's Be Honest About What a Workshop Paper Is (and Isn't)
The Conference Tier Breakdown: Which Workshops Actually Move the Needle
How to Actually List Workshop Papers on Your CV Without Overselling or Underselling
The SOP Section: This Is Where Workshop Papers Either Help or Hurt You
When a Workshop Paper Is a Net Negative on Your Application
The 2026 Context: How the ML PhD Market Has Changed Workshop Paper Value
Practical Checklist: Before You Submit Your 2026 PhD Application
You got accepted to the NeurIPS 2025 Workshop on Robustness in Foundation Models. Congrats — that's genuinely hard. You spent three months on this paper, got real feedback from reviewers, and made it into a venue where serious researchers will see your work. Now you're staring at the "Publications" section of your PhD application and wondering if listing it will help you or silently mark you as someone who doesn't know how academia works. That uncertainty is the actual problem, and it's more common than anyone admits out loud.
The Reddit threads aren't overreacting. I've seen "does a workshop paper count?" posts on r/MachineLearning and r/gradadmissions hit the front page repeatedly, because the anxiety is legitimate — nobody in undergrad explicitly teaches you the informal prestige hierarchy of ML venues. You didn't go to a school with a strong ML lab. Your advisor is in a related field and says "it's good, list it." But you've seen people online say workshop papers are worthless, and other people say they got into top-5 programs with nothing but workshop papers. Both things are true for different people in different situations, which is exactly why generic advice fails here.
Here's my actual take: a workshop paper is a signal amplifier, not a signal by itself. It

zh.title: 研讨会论文真的能帮助你的博士申请吗？这是招生委员会实际看到的
zh.summary: 来自NeurIPS和ICML等会议的研讨会论文可以显著影响博士申请结果，但其价值取决于定位和更广泛的申请背景。研讨会论文充当信号放大器而非独立凭证，强化已展示研究能力的申请，对较弱申请帮助有限。申请者应理解机器学习会议的非正式声望等级，并战略性地定位研讨会论文。

title: How to Optimize Python 3.12 Code with Cython 3 and Rust 1.85 Bindings for 10x Speedups
summary: Python 3.12 code can achieve 10-14x performance improvements through Cython 3 static typing and Rust 1.85 FFI bindings, as demonstrated by matrix multiplication benchmarks showing speedups from 12.7 seconds (pure Python) to 0.89 seconds (Rust). Cython 3 provides full Python 3.12 support with improved GIL release primitives, while Rust's pyo3 crate reduces binding boilerplate by 40%.
content:
Python’s global interpreter lock (GIL) and dynamic typing make it 10–100x slower than compiled languages for CPU-bound workloads. In 2024 benchmarks, a naive Python 3.12 matrix multiplication function took 12.7 seconds to process 1000x1000 float arrays, while a Cython 3-optimized version ran in 1.4 seconds, and a Rust 1.85 binding dropped that to 0.89 seconds — a 14.3x speedup over pure Python, with zero Python code changes for the Rust path.
  ⭐ rust-lang/rust — 112,466 stars, 14,875 forks
  ⭐ python/cpython — 72,543 stars, 34,529 forks
Data pulled live from GitHub and npm.
  Ti-84 Evo (232 points)
  New research suggests people can communicate and practice skills while dreaming (207 points)
  The smelly baby problem (67 points)
  Eka’s robotic claw feels like we're approaching a ChatGPT moment (75 points)
  Ask HN: Who is hiring? (May 2026) (218 points)
  Matrix multiplication workloads see 8–14x speedups when optimized with Cython 3 static typing and Rust 1.85 FFI bindings, benchmarked against Python 3.12.2 on an M2 Max CPU.
  Cython 3.0.5 adds full Python 3.12 support, including match statements and improved GIL release primitives for zero-overhead parallelism.
  Rust 1.85’s pyo3 0.20 crate reduces binding boilerplate by 40% compared to 1.70, with automatic GIL management for Python 3.12 compatibility.
  By 2026, 60% of performance-critical Python libraries will ship Rust or Cython extensions as default, up from 22% in 2023, per PyPI download metrics.
Before optimizing, we need a reproducible baseline to measure gains against. We’ll implement a naive matrix multiplication function in pure Python 3.12, with validation, benchmarking, and correctness checks. This code will run without any external dependencies (beyond the standard library) to isolate Python’s native performance.

import time
import sys
from typing import List, Tuple, Optional

def validate_matrix(mat: List[List[float]], name: str) -> None:
    """Validate that a matrix is non-empty and rectangular.

zh.title: 如何使用Cython 3和Rust 1.85绑定优化Python 3.12代码以实现10倍加速
zh.summary: Python 3.12代码可通过Cython 3静态类型和Rust 1.85 FFI绑定实现10-14倍性能提升，矩阵乘法基准测试证明从12.7秒（纯Python）加速到0.89秒（Rust）。Cython 3提供完整Python 3.12支持并改进了GIL释放原语，Rust的pyo3库将绑定样板代码减少40%。

title: The AI Harness: why your AI coding agent is only as smart as the repo you put it in
summary: An open-source team documented how codebase quality and organization—the 'AI harness'—determines whether AI agents can effectively contribute to real production systems with multiple components and third-party integrations. By implementing proper documentation, clear architecture, consistent patterns, and test infrastructure, the team moved from agents inventing non-existent entities to shipping production-ready features. This reveals that AI agent effectiveness is primarily constrained by repository structure rather than language model capability.
content:
Hi. I am an engineer at Activepieces, an open source automation platform (think Zapier, but you can self host it and read the source code). I write TypeScript for a living. I argue with bots about tabs vs spaces now. And our team spent the last few months really, really invested in one question:
Why does our AI coding agent suck in this codebase, but cook so hard in a blank Next.js project on my laptop?
Spoiler: it is not the model. It is never the model. It is me. It is the repo. It is the thing I will spend the next ~6,000 words trying to convince you to call an AI harness.
Here is the claim. I want to be careful with it, because the internet is tired of "we 10x'd productivity with AI" hype posts. I am tired of them too.
We have 12+ engineers at Activepieces. Our internal goal is one feature per engineer per day, ideally from a single prompt. We have not hit that goal yet. But we are moving fast toward it, and the speed of progress is what is actually interesting.
We are an open source team trying to figure out, in public, how to set up a real codebase so a frontier model can actually be useful in it.
Our codebase is real. Multi tenant. Multi edition. We have an enterprise tier, a cloud product, a frontend, a backend, an engine, a piece SDK, and integrations with hundreds of third party APIs. So this is not a toy.
This post is the answer we have so far. It is not the final answer. It is the answer that took us from "Claude Code keeps inventing entities that do not exist" to "Claude Code just shipped a credentials manager: a solid plan, two iterations, and the feature was production ready."
Let me show you how.
💡 Reading time check: This is a long post. Around 30 minutes. There is a lot of stuff. I would rather give you the full picture than send you off with a half formed mental model that breaks the moment you try to use it. Grab coffee. Or skip to the section called "Anatomy of an AI Harness" if you want the meat.

Let me start with the part of AI coding talk t

zh.title: AI工具库：你的AI编码代理只能与你放入的仓库一样聪明
zh.summary: 一个开源团队记录了代码库质量和组织（"AI工具库"）如何决定AI代理能否有效地为具有多个组件和第三方集成的真实生产系统做出贡献。通过实施适当的文档、清晰的架构、一致的模式和测试基础设施，该团队从代理发明不存在的实体转变为交付生产就绪的功能。这揭示了AI代理的有效性主要由仓库结构而非语言模型能力所制约。

title: Images, Fonts, Third-Party Scripts: LCP and CLS
summary: Images, fonts, and third-party scripts are responsible for the majority of field Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) regressions, with interactions between them amplifying performance degradation. Optimization techniques include using fetchpriority attributes and preload directives for hero images, selecting appropriate image formats, implementing font-display strategies, and using the facade pattern to defer third-party script execution until user interaction.
content: Images, fonts, and third-party scripts are the three categories responsible for the most field LCP and CLS regressions. They interact: a 12KB GTM tag firing synchronously in blocks the parser for 200ms, which means your hero image (already the LCP candidate) now loads 200ms later. LCP jumps from 1.8s to 4.2s. CrUX data reflects it three weeks later. What this covers: fetchpriority and for LCP images, WebP vs AVIF trade-offs, srcset/sizes resolution selection, font-display values and what each costs, font subsetting, and the facade pattern that eliminates third-party script cost entirely until user interaction. Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures when the largest visible element in the viewport finishes rendering. In most marketing sites, e-commerce pages, and landing pages, that element is an image: specifically the hero image above the fold. The browser's default behavior for images is loading="lazy", which means it defers fetching images until they're near the viewport. For images below the fold, this is exactly right. For the LCP image, it is catastrophic. ![Hero](https://renderlog.in/hero.jpg) ![Hero](https://renderlog.in/hero.webp) fetchpriority="high" is the piece most developers miss. Even with loading="eager", the browser's resource prioritization algorithm might assign the image a lower network priority if it's discovered late in the HTML parse: for example, if it's inside a JS-rendered component. fetchpriority="high" overrides this and tells the browser: this resource competes with critical CSS, treat it accordingly. The most aggressive technique is a in the document head. This fires before the HTML parser even reaches the tag:

zh.title: 图片、字体、第三方脚本：LCP 和 CLS
zh.summary: 图片、字体和第三方脚本导致大部分字段最大内容绘制（LCP）和累积布局偏移（CLS）性能下降，它们之间的相互作用进一步加剧了性能恶化。优化技术包括为关键图片使用 fetchpriority 属性和预加载指令、选择适当的图片格式、实施字体显示策略，以及使用外观模式延迟第三方脚本执行直到用户交互。

title: Deploying Blob Storage for a Public website on Azure
summary: Azure Blob Storage provides cloud-based unstructured data storage suitable for public website asset hosting, configured with read-access geo-redundant storage for high availability and anonymous blob-level read access. The setup involves creating a storage account, configuring redundancy settings, creating public containers, and enabling anonymous access permissions. This configuration supports secure public file distribution while maintaining durability through geographic redundancy.
content:
Table of Content



What is Blob Storage?
Create a storage account with high availability.
Create a blob storage container with anonymous read access
Practice uploading files and testing access.
Configure soft delete
Configure blob versioning
Conclusion

Blob Storage is a cloud storage solution used for storing large amount of unstructured data including text documents, emails, social media posts, multimedia (audio/video/images) Learn more.  

In the portal, search for and select Storage accounts.

Select + Create.

For resource group select new. Give your resource group a name and select OK.
Set the Storage account name to publicwebsite. Make sure the storage account name is unique by adding an identifier.
Take the defaults for other settings.
Select Review and then Create.

Wait for the storage account to deploy, and then select Go to resource.

In the storage account, in the Data management section, select the Redundancy blade.
Ensure Read-access Geo-redundant storage is selected.
Review the primary and secondary location information.

In the storage account, in the Settings section, select the Configuration blade.
Ensure the Allow blob anonymous access setting is Enabled.
Be sure to Save your changes.


In your storage account, in the Data storage section, select the Containers blade.
Select + Container.
Ensure the Name of the container is public.
Select Create.

Select your public container.
On the Overview blade, select Change access level.

Ensure the Public access level is Blob (anonymous read access for blobs only).
Select OK.


public container. The type of file doesn’t matter. A small image or text file is a good choice.



Ensure you are viewing your container.
Select Upload.
Browse to files and select a file. Browse to a file of your choice.
Select Upload.
Close the upload window, Refresh the page and ensure your file was uploaded.

Select your uploaded file.
On the Overview tab, copy the URL.

Paste the URL into a new browser tab.
If you have uploaded

title: Taming the Chaos: Cleaning 10M+ Apple Health Records into a Production-Ready Parquet Lakehouse
summary: Transforming Apple Health's bloated XML exports into production-ready formats requires a robust data pipeline handling duplicate entries from multiple devices and inconsistent sampling frequencies across 10+ million records. Using Polars for high-performance DataFrame operations, Apache Hop for metadata-driven workflow orchestration, and S3 for distributed storage creates a scalable architecture that processes data without excessive RAM consumption.
content:
If you’ve ever tried to click that "Export Health Data" button on your iPhone, you know the feeling of pure dread that follows. You expect a clean CSV; you get a bloated, multi-gigabyte XML file that looks like it was designed by a chaotic deity. 
When building high-performance AI models for health tech, Apple Health data is a goldmine—but only if you can navigate the minefield of data engineering challenges. We’re talking about massive data volumes, duplicate entries from overlapping devices (iPhone vs. Apple Watch), and inconsistent sampling frequencies that would make any data scientist cry. 
In this tutorial, we are going to build a robust Data Pipeline using Polars, Apache Hop, and S3 to transform "dirty" XML exports into a standardized, high-performance Parquet Lakehouse. 
Pro-Tip: If you are looking for advanced architectural patterns for health-tech scaling, I highly recommend checking out the production-ready examples over at WellAlly's Engineering Blog.
Before we dive into the code, let's look at the flow. We need a system that can handle millions of rows without blowing up your RAM.
graph TD
    A[Apple Health Export XML] --> B[Apache Hop: Ingestion]
    B --> C[S3 Raw Landing Zone]
    C --> D[Polars: Data Cleaning & De-duplication]
    D --> E[Outlier Removal & Resampling]
    E --> F[Standardized Parquet Lakehouse]
    F --> G[Downstream AI/ML Models]

    subgraph "The Processing Core"
    D
    E
    end

To follow along, you’ll need:
Python 3.9+
Polars: The lightning-fast DataFrame library.
Apache Hop: For workflow orchestration.
S3 Bucket: To act as our data lake storage.
While we love Python, using Apache Hop for the initial ingestion allows us to handle the XML-to-S3 transfer visually and reliably. Hop handles the metadata-driven workflow, ensuring that if the upload fails halfway through, we can resume.
Create a "Get XML" transform.
Use the "S3 File Output" to land the raw XML into an s3://raw-zone/.
Why Polars? Because parsing 10 million rows o

zh.title: 驯服混乱：将 1000 万+ 条 Apple Health 记录清洁为生产就绪的 Parquet Lakehouse
zh.summary: 将 Apple Health 臃肿的 XML 导出转换为生产就绪格式需要强大的数据管道来处理来自多个设备的重复条目和跨 1000 万+ 条记录的不一致采样频率。使用 Polars 进行高性能 DataFrame 操作、Apache Hop 进行元数据驱动的工作流编排以及 S3 进行分布式存储，创建了一个可扩展的架构，能够处理数据而无需过度消耗 RAM。

title: Why Most Crypto Bots Get Sandwiched (And How to Prevent It)
summary: Sandwich attacks in decentralized finance occur when attackers manipulate transaction ordering in the public mempool by front-running a victim's transaction with higher gas fees, executing their own profitable transaction, and back-running after execution. Most crypto trading bots remain vulnerable because they operate in public mempools with predictable behavior and low gas fees, providing attackers sufficient time to detect and exploit opportunities.
content:
Why Most Crypto Bots Get Sandwiched (And How to Prevent It)


If you’ve ever built or run a crypto trading bot, you’ve likely encountered the dreaded "sandwich attack." It’s one of the most frustrating and costly issues in decentralized finance (DeFi) trading. In this article, I’ll explain what sandwich attacks are, how they work, why your bot is vulnerable, and—most importantly—how to protect yourself using tools like Jito bundles. I’ll also share some real-world numbers and practical lessons I’ve learned the hard way.
A sandwich attack is a form of Miner Extractable Value (MEV) where an attacker exploits your transaction by placing their own transaction before and after yours. Here’s how it works:
Front-Running: The attacker detects your pending transaction (e.g., a swap on Uniswap) and submits their own transaction with a higher gas fee to execute first.
Execution: Your transaction executes in the middle, often at a worse price due to the attacker’s manipulation.
Back-Running: The attacker closes their position by executing another transaction immediately after yours, profiting from the price movement they created.
For example, if your bot tries to buy ETH with DAI, an attacker can buy ETH first (driving the price up), allow your transaction to execute at the inflated price, and then sell ETH immediately after, pocketing the difference.
Most crypto bots are vulnerable to sandwich attacks because they operate in public mempools. Transactions sit in the mempool before being included in a block, and attackers use sophisticated tools to scan for profitable opportunities. Here are the key reasons:
Low Gas Fees: Bots often use low or average gas fees to save costs, making them susceptible to being front-run.
Predictable Behavior: Bots often follow simple strategies (e.g., buying when a price hits a threshold), making their transactions easy to anticipate.
Limited Protection: Many bot developers don’t implement MEV protection mechanisms, leaving their transactions expos

zh.title: 为什么大多数加密机器人容易遭受夹击攻击（以及如何防止）
zh.summary: 去中心化金融中的夹击攻击是指攻击者通过提高燃气费用抢先执行受害者交易，然后执行自己的有利可图的交易，最后进行回追的方式来操纵公共内存池中的交易顺序。大多数加密交易机器人易于被攻击，因为它们在具有可预测行为和低燃气费用的公共内存池中运行，给攻击者充足的时间来检测和利用机会。

title: Devstral 2: Run Mistral's Open Coding Agent Locally
summary: Mistral's Devstral 2 achieves 72.2% accuracy on SWE-bench Verified—matching Claude Sonnet 4.5's performance—while running entirely on-premise on a single RTX 4090 GPU or Mac with 32GB RAM. The model costs 7x less per token than Claude Sonnet 4.5 and was trained to excel at real multi-file, multi-step engineering tasks. This approach trades five percentage points of accuracy for 85% cost savings.
content:
Proprietary coding agents are powerful, but every token you send to a cloud API is a token that leaves your codebase. Devstral 2, released December 9, 2025 by Mistral AI, changes that calculus. At 72.2% on SWE-bench Verified — the industry-standard benchmark for real GitHub issue resolution — it matches Claude Sonnet 4.5's general performance territory while running fully on-premise on a single RTX 4090 or a Mac with 32GB RAM.
This guide covers what Devstral 2 and Devstral Small 2 are, their specs and benchmarks, how to pull and run the smaller variant via Ollama, and how to wire either model into your daily coding workflow.
Effloow Lab verified Ollama compatibility (v0.20.5), confirmed registry tags, and documented hardware requirements before writing this guide. See data/lab-runs/devstral-2-mistral-coding-agent-local-guide-2026.md for the full evidence record.
Most open-weight coding models have a dirty secret: they score well on toy benchmarks but fall apart on real multi-file, multi-step engineering tasks. SWE-bench Verified uses 500 manually screened GitHub issues from production repositories — not hand-curated puzzles. It measures whether an agent can actually read a codebase, reason about the bug, make targeted edits across multiple files, and produce a passing test run.
Devstral 2's 72.2% on that benchmark is not a coincidence. Mistral built the model in collaboration with All Hands AI, the team behind OpenHands — a production agentic framework used for real software engineering workflows. The training process was specifically designed around multi-step tool use: reading files, editing code, running tests, and iterating based on output.
For teams running high-volume coding tasks, the cost arithmetic is compelling. Claude Sonnet 4.5, which scores 77.2% on the same benchmark, costs $3/$15 per million tokens (input/output). Devstral 2 API pricing is $0.40/$2.00 — roughly 7x cheaper per token. Trading five percentage points of accuracy for 85% cost savings is a

zh.title: Devstral 2：在本地运行Mistral的开源编码代理
zh.summary: Mistral的Devstral 2在SWE-bench Verified上达到72.2%的准确率——与Claude Sonnet 4.5性能相当——同时完全本地运行，支持单个RTX 4090 GPU或32GB RAM的Mac。该模型的每个token成本比Claude Sonnet 4.5低7倍，专门为多文件、多步骤工程任务而训练。此方案以牺牲5个百分点的准确率换取85%的成本节省。

title: OpenTelemetry 1.25 vs. Datadog 2026: Tracing Overhead for 1000 RPS Microservices Workloads Measured
summary: A benchmark of 1000 RPS microservices workloads shows OpenTelemetry 1.25 introduces 35-45% lower tracing overhead compared to Datadog 2026 across latency, CPU, and memory metrics. Both systems maintained >99.98% trace export success rates under sustained load, though OpenTelemetry achieved slightly lower export latency. These results demonstrate that distributed tracing overhead is quantifiable and tool selection significantly impacts production microservices performance.
content:
OpenTelemetry 1.25 vs Datadog 2026: Tracing Overhead for 1000 RPS Microservices Workloads


Distributed tracing is critical for debugging microservices, but instrumentation overhead can degrade production performance. This benchmark compares OpenTelemetry (OTel) 1.25 and Datadog’s 2026 tracing stack under a sustained 1000 requests per second (RPS) microservices workload to quantify real-world overhead.
We deployed a 3-service e-commerce microservices stack on Kubernetes (1.29):
  Frontend: Node.js 20, handles user requests
  Backend: Go 1.22, processes business logic
  Database: PostgreSQL 16, persists data
Load was generated via k6 at a constant 1000 RPS for 60 minutes. We measured overhead with 100% trace sampling to isolate instrumentation impact, with no other observability tools running. Key metrics:
  Request latency (p50, p95, p99) with and without tracing
  Per-pod CPU and memory utilization
  Trace export success rate and export latency
We used the OTel SDK for Node.js and Go, with the OTLP gRPC exporter sending traces to a local OpenTelemetry Collector 0.90. Sampling was set to 100% (always_on). No additional processors or extensions were enabled to minimize external overhead.
We installed the Datadog Agent 7.55 (2026 GA release) with tracing enabled. The Datadog Node.js and Go tracing libraries were used, with 100% sampling matching the OTel configuration. Default Datadog tagging (service, env, version) was left enabled.
All tests were run 3 times, with results averaged. Baseline (no tracing) latency: p50=12ms, p95=45ms, p99=89ms.

  OpenTelemetry 1.25: 99.992% export success, average export latency 12ms
  Datadog 2026: 99.989% export success, average export latency 18ms
OpenTelemetry 1.25 showed 35-45% lower overhead across all metrics. This aligns with

zh.title: OpenTelemetry 1.25 vs. Datadog 2026：1000 RPS微服务工作负载追踪开销测量
zh.summary: 对1000 RPS微服务工作负载的基准测试显示，OpenTelemetry 1.25在延迟、CPU和内存指标上相比Datadog 2026的追踪开销降低35-45%。两个系统在持续负载下都保持99.98%以上的追踪导出成功率，尽管OpenTelemetry的导出延迟略低。这些结果表明分布式追踪开销可量化，工具选择对生产微服务性能影响重大。
